title: Reversible reactions and equilibrium — when forward and backward happen at the same time
examTip: Le Chatelier's principle: if you stress an equilibrium system, it shifts to OPPOSE that change. Temperature shifts in the endothermic direction when raised.
introTips: The ⇌ symbol is the key — it tells you the reaction can go both ways.
Dynamic equilibrium means BOTH reactions are still happening — rates are equal, not amounts.
The Haber process is the go-to exam example — know the conditions and the compromises.
intro: Most reactions in chemistry textbooks look like a one-way street — reactants go in, products come out. But in real life, many reactions are more like a revolving door. The products can turn back into reactants just as easily as the reactants form products. This back-and-forth is called a reversible reaction, and when it reaches a kind of balance — called equilibrium — it's one of the most powerful ideas in chemistry. The Haber process, which makes the fertiliser that feeds billions of people, is built entirely on controlling equilibrium.

keyPoints.heading: Reversible reactions
keyPoints.content: A reversible reaction is one that can proceed in both directions: forward (reactants → products) and backward (products → reactants). It is shown by the ⇌ symbol instead of →. Example: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (the Haber process). Another key AQA example: heating blue hydrated copper sulfate (CuSO₄·5H₂O) produces white anhydrous copper sulfate (CuSO₄) + water. Adding water reverses this — the white powder turns blue again. This is a classic test: anhydrous copper sulfate as a test for water (turns blue). A reaction is reversible if the products can be converted back into the original reactants under appropriate conditions.
writeBullets: Reversible reaction — can proceed in both the forward AND backward direction
Shown by the ⇌ symbol in the equation
Forward reaction: reactants → products
Backward reaction: products → reactants
AQA example: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue) ⇌ CuSO₄ (white) + 5H₂O — heat drives forward, water reverses it
Test for water: white anhydrous copper sulfate turns blue in the presence of water

keyTerms.def: A reaction that can proceed in both the forward and backward directions, shown by the ⇌ symbol.
A substance that contains no water (e.g., anhydrous copper sulfate is the white form with no water of crystallisation).
A substance that contains water of crystallisation within its crystal structure (e.g., hydrated copper sulfate is blue).

keyPoints.memTip: Blue → white → blue. Heat to dehydrate (remove water), add water to rehydrate. The ⇌ arrow tells you it can go both ways.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The copper sulfate example is a required AQA practical link and frequently appears in exams. Students should know the colours, the conditions to drive each direction, and that it also serves as a water test.

keyPoints.heading: Dynamic equilibrium
keyPoints.content: In a closed system (nothing can enter or leave), a reversible reaction eventually reaches dynamic equilibrium. At equilibrium: the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction. The concentrations of reactants and products are constant — but NOT necessarily equal (there can be more products than reactants or vice versa). The system is called 'dynamic' because both reactions are still occurring — it is NOT static. The concentrations are constant because the two rates balance each other. If the system is open (products can escape), equilibrium may not be reached because the reverse reaction has no products to work with.
writeBullets: Dynamic equilibrium — reached in a closed system when forward rate = reverse rate
Concentrations of reactants and products are constant — but NOT necessarily equal
Both reactions are still occurring simultaneously — the system is 'dynamic'
The word 'equilibrium' describes the balance of rates, not the amounts of substances
Closed system required — if products escape, equilibrium is not established
Open system example: burning fuel — products (CO₂, H₂O) escape so no equilibrium

keyTerms.def: The state in a closed system where the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, so concentrations remain constant even though both reactions continue.
A system in which no matter can enter or leave — a requirement for equilibrium to be established.

keyPoints.memTip: Dynamic = still moving. Equilibrium = balanced. Think of an escalator with equal numbers going up and down — the crowd size on each floor stays the same even though people are still moving.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The most common error: students say concentrations are 'equal' at equilibrium. They are constant, not equal. Push this distinction every time.

keyPoints.heading: Le Chatelier's principle — position of equilibrium
keyPoints.content: Le Chatelier's principle: if conditions of an equilibrium system are changed, the system shifts to oppose the change. The 'position of equilibrium' describes whether the system favours the forward or reverse reaction. Temperature: increase → equilibrium shifts in the endothermic direction (uses up the extra energy). Decrease → shifts in the exothermic direction. Pressure (gases only): increase → equilibrium shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas. Decrease → shifts toward more moles of gas. Concentration: increase concentration of a reactant → equilibrium shifts forward (toward products). Increase concentration of a product → shifts backward. Remove a product → equilibrium shifts forward (system tries to replace it).
writeBullets: Le Chatelier's principle — equilibrium shifts to OPPOSE any change in conditions
Temperature increase → shifts in endothermic direction (absorbs extra energy)
Temperature decrease → shifts in exothermic direction (releases energy to compensate)
Pressure increase → shifts toward side with FEWER moles of gas
Pressure decrease → shifts toward side with MORE moles of gas
Concentration increase of reactant → shifts forward; removing product → also shifts forward

keyTerms.def: If conditions are changed for a system at equilibrium, the equilibrium shifts in the direction that opposes the change.
Whether the equilibrium favours the forward reaction (toward products) or the reverse reaction (toward reactants).
The direction of reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings.
The direction of reaction that releases energy to the surroundings.

keyPoints.memTip: Le Chatelier = the equilibrium FIGHTS BACK. Push it one way and it pushes back. Raise temperature → it cools down by going endothermic. Raise pressure → it shrinks by making fewer gas molecules.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'shifts toward fewer moles of gas' when pressure increases. Make sure they count gas moles on each side — sometimes both sides have the same number, in which case pressure has no effect.

keyPoints.heading: The Haber process — a real example of equilibrium control
keyPoints.content: The Haber process makes ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. The forward reaction is exothermic (ΔH negative). Counting gas moles: 4 moles on the left (1 N₂ + 3 H₂), 2 moles on the right (2 NH₃). Industrial conditions: approximately 450°C temperature, approximately 200 atmospheres pressure, iron catalyst. Compromise reasoning: lower temperature → more NH₃ at equilibrium (exothermic favoured) but reaction is too slow; 450°C is the compromise between yield and speed. Higher pressure → more NH₃ (fewer moles of gas favoured) but high pressure is expensive and dangerous; 200 atm is the compromise. Iron catalyst speeds up the reaction but does NOT affect the equilibrium position. Unreacted N₂ and H₂ are recycled back into the reactor to reduce waste.
writeBullets: Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ — exothermic forward reaction
4 moles of gas → 2 moles of gas, so higher pressure favours more NH₃
Industrial conditions: ~450°C, ~200 atm, iron catalyst
450°C compromise: lower T gives more NH₃ but too slow; higher T is faster but less NH₃
200 atm compromise: higher P gives more NH₃ but too expensive and dangerous
Iron catalyst speeds up equilibrium being reached but doesn't change the yield
Unreacted N₂ and H₂ are recycled to minimise waste

keyTerms.def: The industrial process for making ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, using conditions of ~450°C, ~200 atm, and an iron catalyst.
Industrial conditions chosen to balance yield and rate of reaction — neither the best for yield alone nor the best for speed alone.

keyPoints.memTip: HABER = Hot (450°C), Atmosphere (200 atm), Big iron catalyst, Exothermic, Recycled gases.
keyPoints.tutor_note: A very common 6-mark question: 'Explain why these conditions are used in the Haber process.' Students must address temperature, pressure, and catalyst with both 'why not more' and 'why not less' for the temperature and pressure choices.

tables.title: Le Chatelier's principle — effect of changing conditions
rows: Increase temperature
Endothermic direction
Equilibrium absorbs extra energy by shifting in the endothermic direction
Forward reaction is exothermic → increasing T shifts equilibrium BACKWARD → less NH₃
Decrease temperature
Exothermic direction
Equilibrium releases energy to compensate
Decreasing T shifts equilibrium FORWARD → more NH₃, but reaction is too slow
Increase pressure
Side with fewer moles of gas
Reduces the volume occupied by gas molecules, opposing the pressure increase
4 mol gas → 2 mol gas; increasing P shifts forward → more NH₃
Decrease pressure
Side with more moles of gas
Increases volume, opposing the pressure decrease
Decreasing P shifts backward → less NH₃
Increase concentration of reactant
Forward (toward products)
Uses up added reactant, opposing the change
Adding more N₂ or H₂ shifts equilibrium forward → more NH₃
Remove product (e.g., ammonia)
Forward (toward products)
Equilibrium replaces the removed product
In Haber process, NH₃ is removed by liquefaction → drives equilibrium forward

1.question: What does 'dynamic' mean in the term 'dynamic equilibrium'?
options: Both the forward and reverse reactions are still occurring at equal rates
The concentrations of reactants and products are equal
Only the forward reaction is happening
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. 'Dynamic' means both reactions are still happening — it's the rates that are equal, not the concentrations.
1.wrongFeedback: Dynamic means the reactions are still going in both directions — forward AND backward. The rates are equal, so concentrations don't change. The system is balanced but not stopped.

2.question: The Haber process uses a temperature of ~450°C rather than a much lower temperature. Why?
options: A lower temperature would produce more ammonia but the reaction would be too slow to be economical
A lower temperature would produce less ammonia and would also be slower
A higher temperature produces more ammonia and a faster rate
The iron catalyst only works above 450°C
2.correctFeedback: Correct — 450°C is a compromise. Lower temperatures would favour the exothermic forward reaction (more NH₃) but the reaction is too slow. 450°C gives an acceptable yield at an acceptable rate.
2.wrongFeedback: Remember: the Haber process forward reaction is exothermic. Lower temperature shifts equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier) — more NH₃. But too low a temperature means the rate is too slow. 450°C is the compromise.

3.question: In the reaction A + B ⇌ C + D, if you increase the concentration of A, which way does the equilibrium shift?
options: Forward — toward products C and D
Backward — toward reactants A and B
No shift — concentration doesn't affect equilibrium
3.correctFeedback: Spot on. Adding more reactant A shifts equilibrium forward to use it up — Le Chatelier's principle in action.
3.wrongFeedback: Le Chatelier's principle: if you increase the concentration of a reactant, the equilibrium shifts forward to use up the extra reactant and produce more products.

commonMistakes: Saying concentrations are 'equal' at equilibrium — they are constant, not equal. There can be far more products than reactants or vice versa.
Forgetting that a catalyst does NOT shift the equilibrium position — it only speeds up how quickly equilibrium is reached.
Saying temperature increase always shifts equilibrium forward — it shifts in the endothermic direction, which could be forward OR backward depending on the reaction.
Not counting gas moles correctly when applying pressure — always count total moles of gas on each side before deciding which way the equilibrium shifts.

Saying the Haber process uses low temperature to get a fast rate — low temperature actually slows the rate; 450°C is a compromise between yield (which prefers lower T) and rate (which prefers higher T).
examTips: For Le Chatelier questions: state the direction of shift, then explain WHY in terms of what the system is opposing.
For Haber process questions: always explain BOTH why a more extreme condition isn't used AND what the compromise achieves.
Remember: catalysts speed up attainment of equilibrium but do NOT change the equilibrium position or the yield.
revisionCardBullets: Reversible reaction: can go both forward AND backward; shown by ⇌
Dynamic equilibrium: in a closed system, forward rate = reverse rate; concentrations constant but not necessarily equal
Le Chatelier's principle: equilibrium shifts to OPPOSE any change in conditions
Temperature increase → shifts in endothermic direction
Pressure increase → shifts toward fewer moles of gas
Concentration increase of reactant → shifts forward (toward products)
Haber conditions: ~450°C (compromise — yield vs rate), ~200 atm (compromise — yield vs cost/safety), iron catalyst
Catalyst in Haber: speeds up reaching equilibrium; does NOT affect equilibrium position
Unreacted N₂ and H₂ are recycled in Haber process
Ammonia removed by liquefaction, which drives equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier)
